label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's read the passage and answer the question. The passage describes some strange baby-naming laws in different countries. The question is asking which of the given options is true according to the passage. Let me analyze each option: (A) Danish babies' names should be on the list if parents can't get the permission. This seems to be the opposite of what's stated in the passage. The passage says "If Danish parents prefer a moniker not on the list of 7,000 preapproved baby names, they must get permission from local church and government officials." So if they can't get permission, the name wouldn't be accepted, not that they should use a name from the list if they can't get permission. The statement in option A is not supported by the passage. (B) Each year about 150-200 reviewed names are rejected in Denmark. The passage says "Fifteen to 20 percent of the 1,100 reviewed names... are rejected each year." So 15-20% of 1,100 is 165-220 names. This is approximately 150-200 names (though technically the higher end is 220). This option is close enough to what the passage says. (C) Adolf Hitler is banned in Iceland. The passage says "Adolf Hitler and Yeah Detroit--both names recently rejected" but this refers to New Zealand, not Iceland. So this option is false according to the passage. (D) Parents should pay a fee for babies' names if the names are rejected. The passage says "If parents want to go off-list, they must apply for approval and pay a fee" in Iceland. But this is about applying for an off-list name, not about paying a fee if the name is rejected. There's no mention of a fee for rejected names. So this
